author: potuz
content:
This is flawed:
> However, if the payment met quorum before eviction, epoch processing already appended payment.withdrawal to state.builder_pending_withdrawals.
There is no possibility of achieving quorum if there hasn't been any blocks from the parent until this block. Quorum is on-chain and no attestations could have been processed

author: potuz
content: I stopped reading here, but I assume you can get an AI to vet that the PoC starts with a flawed state in which the quorum is applied before the epoch transition, which I claim it's impossible

author: potuz
content: also notice that in general, quorum is irrelevant for a payload that **has been included**, the payment processed in this function is for payloads that have been included and thus we just charge   the builder

author: nflaig
content:
I just asked claude opus 4.8 whether the report is valid or not and it found the flaw
```
  Why it can't actually happen

  The double payment requires a single state where payment[P] both (a) reached quorum (so epoch settlement paid it) and (b) has P as the immediate parent that is ≥2 epochs stale (so the
  direct-append branch fires). Those two are mutually exclusive on any one chain history:

  - latest_execution_payload_bid is written in exactly one place — process_execution_payload_bid (beacon-chain.md:1590), once per block, to that block's own bid. So parent_bid.slot is always
  the slot of the immediately preceding canonical block. For the elif to fire, that preceding block must be P and must be 2+ epochs old ⇒ a 2+ epoch gap of empty slots between P and the
  current block.
  - Weight on payment[P] only comes from same-slot attestations for slot P, which are added by process_attestation — and that only runs inside process_block. Those attestations must be
  included in blocks built on top of P, during epochs E/E+1 (process_attestation rejects target.epoch older than previous: beacon-chain.md:1751).

  The clean way to see the contradiction: a block built directly on P inherits P's post-state, in which payment[P] has weight = 0 (attestations for P are only ever added by later blocks,
  which would then be the parent instead of P). So whenever the elif parent_bid.value > 0 branch fires, the evicted entry provably had weight 0 → epoch settlement never paid it → the direct
  append is the sole payment. Conversely, any block that gave payment[P] quorum weight becomes the new parent, so P is never the stale immediate parent. The two branches partition cleanly;
  no entry is paid twice.

```

author: mushow0x
content:
Also quick question but what's the rationale behind the 256 here? 

[Here](https://github.com/ethereum/consensus-specs/blob/master/specs/gloas/beacon-chain.md?plain=1#L218) : `MAX_BUILDER_DEPOSIT_REQUESTS_PER_PAYLOAD    uint64(2**8) (= 256)`

In the EL [EIP 8282](https://eips.ethereum.org/EIPS/eip-8282) the deposit cap seems to be `MAX_DEPOSIT_REQUESTS_PER_BLOCK = 64`, and the builder exit constant on the CL side (`MAX_BUILDER_EXIT_REQUESTS_PER_PAYLOAD = 16`) matches its EIP counterpart (`MAX_EXIT_REQUESTS_PER_BLOCK = 16`)
So the deposit side is the odd one out at 4x the EIP value?

 The EL EIP also gives a security rationale for that cap, *see screenshot below.*

So is the CL's `256` intentionally a looser wire-format ceiling (relying on the EL to enforce the `64` drain cap, so a valid payload never actually exceeds `64`), or should it track `64` to match the per-block verification bound the EIP describes? 
Just want to understand the reasoning.
